2024

What happends when we don’t know \(\sigma_X\)?

  • The Normal test requires that we know \(\sigma_X\).

  • In practice, we must almost always estimate \(\sigma_X\) from the data.

  • \(s_X\) is a good estimator of \(\sigma_X\)

\[ \begin{align} s_X &= \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})\\\\ \widehat{\sigma_X} &= s_X \end{align} \]

Tempting to plug \(\widehat{\sigma_X}\) into a Normal test

  • \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_X, \sigma_X^2)\): Good
  • \(\bar{X} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_X, \frac{\sigma_X^2}{n})\): Good
  • \(\bar{X} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_X, \frac{\widehat{\sigma_X}^2}{n})\): Bad!

Estimating the variance turns a Normal into a t

\[ \begin{align} X &\sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_X, \sigma_X) \\ Z &= \frac{X - \mu_X}{\sigma_X} \\ Z &\sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \\\\ X &\sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_X, \sigma_X) \\ t &= \frac{X - \mu_X}{\widehat{\sigma_X}} \\ t &\sim t(df) \\ df &= n - 1 \end{align} \]

Estimating variance adds uncertainty

  • The t-distribution has heavier tails than the Normal distribution.

The one-sample t-Test: outline

  • Null Hypothesis: \(\mu_X = c\)

  • Alternative Hypothesis: \(\mu_X \neq c\), \(\mu_X > c\), or \(\mu_X < c\)

  • \(\alpha = 0.05\)

  • Test Statistic: \(t = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\widehat{\sigma_X} / \sqrt{n}} \sim t(df=n-1)\)

  • P-value and Critical Values: Can be obtained from pt() and qt() functions in R.

The one-sample t-Test: visualised

Normal vs t: P-Values

  • Higher tails \(\rightarrow\) larger p-values \(\rightarrow\) less evidence against \(H_0\).

Normal vs t: Critical Values

  • Higher tails \(\rightarrow\) larger p-values \(\rightarrow\) less evidence against \(H_0\).

t-test full example

  • Suppose we are interested in testing whether a neuron increases its firing rate in response to a perihperal stimulus.

  • Let \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_X, \sigma_X)\) be the random variable that generates the firing rate of the neuron.

\[ \begin{align} H_0: &\ \mu_X = 10 \\ H_1: &\ \mu_X > 10 \\[2ex] \alpha &= 0.05 \\[2ex] t_{obs} &= \frac{\bar{X}_{obs} - \mu_{X_{H_0}}}{\widehat{\sigma}_X / \sqrt{n}} \\ &\sim t(df=n-1) \end{align} \]

  • Suppose we observe the following firing rates:
##  [1] 13.448164 11.719628 11.801543 11.221365  9.888318 14.573826 11.995701
##  [8]  7.066766 12.402712 10.054417

  • The observed test statistic (\(t_{obs}\)):
t_obs <- (mean(x_obs) - mu_x) / (sd(x_obs) / sqrt(n))
  • The p-value and critical value:
p_value <- pt(t_obs, df=n-1, lower.tail=F)
t_crit <- qt(0.05, df=n-1, lower.tail=F)

  • The decision:
if (p_value < 0.05) {
  decision <- "Reject H0"
} else {
  decision <- "Fail to reject H0"
}
decision
## [1] "Reject H0"

  • Full visualisation
